content.text: Agents fail in ways that differ fundamentally from traditional software. A function either raises an exception or returns a value. An agent can raise an exception, return a plausible-sounding but wrong answer, silently loop forever consuming tokens, or call the right tool with inputs that produce garbage — with no exception raised in any of these cases. Systematic failure analysis means classifying every failure mode before it occurs, instrumenting the agent loop to detect them as they happen, and aggregating observations so patterns surface across many runs.
Agent failures fall into four root categories. Understanding the category determines the correct response — retry, abort, or validate.
— the API call itself fails. API errors (4xx/5xx), context window overflow, or content filter rejection. Raises an exception the agent loop can catch.
— the LLM call succeeds, the tool is called, but execution fails. Network timeout, malformed input arguments, or the tool return value cannot be parsed as expected.

— both LLM and tool execute without exception but the agent reasons incorrectly: calling the same tool repeatedly in a loop, choosing the wrong tool for the task, or treating a hallucinated capability as real.

— the agent produces a final answer that is either empty, too short to be useful, or factually wrong. No exception is raised; without output validation, the caller receives a bad result and cannot tell.
Classify failures at the point they are detected, attach a severity, and store enough context to diagnose later. A simple dataclass pair covers most agent loops:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Any, Optional
import time


class FailureCategory(Enum):
    LLM_ERROR = "llm_error"
    TOOL_ERROR = "tool_error"
    LOGIC_ERROR = "logic_error"
    SILENT_FAILURE = "silent_failure"


class Severity(Enum):
    FATAL = "fatal"          # agent must stop; human intervention needed
    RECOVERABLE = "recoverable"  # retry with backoff may succeed
    DEGRADED = "degraded"    # completed but output is wrong or empty


@dataclass
class FailureReport:
    category: FailureCategory
    severity: Severity
    message: str
    timestamp: float = field(default_factory=time.time)
    tool_name: Optional[str] = None
    tool_input: Optional[dict] = None
    raw_exception: Optional[str] = None
    context: dict = field(default_factory=dict)

    def is_retryable(self) -> bool:
        return self.severity == Severity.RECOVERABLE

    def summary(self) -> str:
        parts = [f"[{self.severity.value.upper()}] {self.category.value}"]
        if self.tool_name:
            parts.append(f"tool={self.tool_name}")
        parts.append(self.message[:120])
        return " | ".join(parts)
A single classify function maps from a caught exception (or a suspicious-but-not-exceptional situation) to a
. The HTTP status code and exception type carry the most signal for LLM and tool errors; logic and silent failures are detected by inspecting the agent state itself.

Logic errors do not raise exceptions. They must be detected by inspecting the sequence of tool calls accumulated during the agent loop. Two patterns are most common: an identical tool call repeated more than N times in a row, and a total tool-call count exceeding a hard cap.

Silent failures are only detectable after the agent returns. A validation function inspects the final output against task-specific constraints. Common checks: minimum length, absence of uncertainty markers, presence of required fields.

Wire all three detection layers — classify on exception, detect logic errors after each tool call, validate output at the end — into a single instrumented agent loop. The loop collects all failures into a list and decides whether to retry, abort, or return a degraded result.

A single run's failures are useful for immediate retry decisions. Across many runs, count by category and severity to spot systemic problems — a tool that always times out, or a task type that always produces degraded output.
The correct response depends on both category and severity. This table summarises the canonical action for each combination:
Exponential backoff, retry up to N times
Abort, alert operator, check prompt length
Return error string to LLM, let it retry with different params
Abort, log full tool_history for post-mortem
Flag result, optionally rerun with refined prompt

Tool errors are not always exceptions. A tool can return successfully with content that is structurally wrong — a JSON field missing, a list that should have items but is empty, or a numeric value out of range. Validate at the boundary before feeding the result back into the LLM context:
For failed runs that warrant investigation, dump the full tool call history and failure report list together. Pairing them reveals the exact sequence of events that led to each failure:
— accidentally catches KeyboardInterrupt and SystemExit. Always catch
— context overflow will not resolve on retry. Classify before deciding whether to retry.
three times with different queries is not a loop. Compare both name and input.
— a valid answer to 'What is 2+2?' is four characters. Tune
— silent failures are the hardest to track. Always store
even when the run appears to have completed.
— always cap retries with exponential backoff. A rate limit will not clear if all your agents hammer the API every 100ms.
Agent failures fall into four categories: LLM Error, Tool Error, Logic Error, Silent Failure.
Each failure has a severity (FATAL / RECOVERABLE / DEGRADED) that determines the correct response: abort, retry, or flag.
LLM errors are detected on exception; logic errors by inspecting the tool call history; silent failures by validating the final output.
instances including DEGRADED ones so the
can surface systemic patterns across many runs.
Save a post-mortem JSON on fatal failures: full tool history plus all failure reports in one file.
